reasoning: National average deposit rates tend to lag both policy rates (Fed Funds) and market rates (Treasury yields) because they are 'sticky'. Assuming the dates represent a shifted 2023 scenario where the 12-month CD rate averaged around 1.68%, the prevailing environment was characterized by a peak in Fed rates over the preceding months. Consequently, momentum and lagging competitive pressures among banks typically lead to continued, albeit slow, increases in the national average in the immediate near term (supporting a probability > 0.5 for the first resolution date). However, as expectations of rate cuts price into the market (leading to falling 1-year and 10-year Treasury yields), banks adapt to these lower wholesale rates and begin easing their retail CD rates. This makes it less likely that the CD rate will still be strictly higher by the longer-term resolution date, pulling the probability down closer to or slightly below 0.5 as uncertainty increases.
